script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Morning, Sarah. June 20th already. Is it just me, or does time move differently when you have a baby?
script.ja: サラさん、おはようございます。もう6月20日ですね。赤ちゃんがいると、時間の流れって違う気がしませんか？

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It does. You'll get used to it. Ready for The Daily Tanu-chan Show?
script.ja: そうね。慣れるわよ。ザ・デイリー・タヌちゃんショー、準備OK？

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Yeah, I think so. Just trying to remember what I was going to talk about. My brain's a bit scattered after last night.
script.ja: はい、たぶん。何を話そうとしてたか思い出そうとしてるんですけど。昨夜から頭が少し散らかってて。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Another rough night?
script.ja: また大変な夜だった？

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Pretty much. Baby was up at 3. And then 5. Just fed him and went back to sleep. I'm already on my second coffee, maybe third soon.
script.ja: まあそんな感じです。赤ちゃんが3時に起きて、それから5時にも。ミルクあげて、また寝たんですけどね。もう2杯目のコーヒーで、もうすぐ3杯目かな。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: That's normal for about two years. Mine did that. It passes.
script.ja: それは2年くらい普通よ。うちの子もそうだった。そのうちなくなるわ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Two years? Wow. Feels like a long time. I jump at everything these days. Pretty sure it's the baby. Like, any sudden noise, I jump.
script.ja: 2年ですか？うわあ。長いなあ。最近は何にでも飛び起きちゃいます。きっと赤ちゃんが原因でしょうね。例えば、どんな突然の物音にもビクッとするんですよ。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: You're constantly on alert. That's a parent's default setting for a while.
script.ja: 常に警戒しているのね。それがしばらくの間、親のデフォルト設定よ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Yeah, that's it. It’s tough. Anyway, it's really coming down out there today, isn't it?
script.ja: ええ、まさにそれです。大変ですよ。ところで、今日、外はすごい雨ですね。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It is. Moderate rain all day, 100% chance. High of 25.8°C, low of 21.0°C. You definitely needed that umbrella today.
script.ja: そうね。一日中しとしと雨で、降水確率100%だって。最高気温25.8度、最低気温21.0度。今日は傘、絶対必要だったわね。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Yeah, I saw that. So glad I brought my umbrella. Wish I could just stay inside with another coffee and... you know, not jump at every little sound. Or any sound, really.
script.ja: ええ、見ました。傘持ってきて本当によかった。コーヒー飲みながら家でゆっくりして、その…どんな小さな音にも飛び起きないでいたいですよ。というか、どんな音にも。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: I can imagine. I'm having hot tea today, much calmer. No sudden jolts.
script.ja: 想像できるわ。私は今日、ホットティーにしているの。ずっと落ち着くわよ。急な衝撃もないし。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Sounds good. Speaking of babies, I was reading something, actually, about new parents. A report from ZERO TO THREE. It was pretty eye-opening.
script.ja: いいですね。赤ちゃんの話なんですけど、そういえば、新しい親について何か読んでたんです。ZERO TO THREEっていうところからのレポートで。かなり目から鱗でした。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Oh, that's an important organization. What did it say?
script.ja: あら、そこは重要な組織ね。どんなことが書いてあったの？

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Yeah. They said 41% of low-income families saw earnings decline in 2021. That's a huge number. And access to childcare is still a huge challenge.
script.ja: ええ。2021年に低所得世帯の41%が収入減少を経験したって。これってすごい数ですよね。あと、保育園へのアクセスがまだ大きな課題だって。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: That's understandable. It's always been a struggle for many families, especially with childcare costs and availability.
script.ja: それは理解できるわ。特に保育費用や利用可能性を考えると、多くの家族にとって、常に大変なことだったから。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Exactly. And it also said the mental and physical health of babies and toddlers continues to be *jeopardized* by the devastating impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic.
script.ja: まさしく。それから、赤ちゃんや幼児の心身の健康が、COVID-19パンデミックによる壊滅的な影響で、『jeopardized』され続けているって。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Jeopardized means put at risk, or put in danger.
script.ja: jeopardizedは、危険にさらされる、危うくするってことよ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Ah, right. So, like, their health is really in danger because of everything that happened. That's a strong word to use in a report.
script.ja: ああ、なるほど。つまり、起こったこと全てのせいで彼らの健康が本当に危ない、と。レポートで使うには強い言葉ですね。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It is. The report is probably highlighting the severity to get attention.
script.ja: そうね。レポートはたぶん、深刻さを強調して注目を集めようとしているのよ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Makes sense. And the report also mentioned economic and care *disparities* among families. What's a 'disparity'?
script.ja: 納得です。それからレポートでは、家族間の経済的およびケアの『disparities』についても触れていました。『disparity』って何でしたっけ？

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It means a great difference, especially an unfair one, between two things or groups.
script.ja: それは、特に不公平な、二つのものやグループ間の大きな違いのことよ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: I see. So, like, some families have more resources and some have less, creating unfair gaps. It's not just about money, but also access to support.
script.ja: なるほど。つまり、一部の家族はより多くのリソースを持っていて、一部は少ない、不公平な格差を生み出している、と。お金だけでなく、サポートへのアクセスも関係しているんですね。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Yes. It's a persistent problem that the pandemic likely worsened, especially for vulnerable families.
script.ja: ええ。パンデミックがさらに悪化させた可能性のある、根深い問題よ。特に弱い立場にある家族にとってはね。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: It really is. I can totally feel the 'mental and emotional distress' part, even though we're lucky. Sleep deprivation is a real thing. And the constant worry about doing everything right.
script.ja: 本当にそうですね。私たちは恵まれているとはいえ、『精神的、感情的な苦痛』の部分はすごく感じます。睡眠不足って本当に大変です。それに、全てをちゃんとやっているかどうかの絶え間ない心配も。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: It is. But you're doing well. Just keep going. The early years are tough, but they're also precious and they pass quickly.
script.ja: そうね。でも、あなたはよくやってるわよ。このまま頑張って。最初の数年は大変だけど、かけがえのない時間でもあるし、あっという間に過ぎるものよ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Thanks, Sarah. I'm trying. Sometimes I feel like I'm just running on fumes. Like a car almost out of gas, heading for a cliff.
script.ja: ありがとう、サラさん。頑張ってます。時々、もう限界って感じなんですけど。ガソリン切れ寸前の車が崖に向かってるみたいに。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: That's how it is. Mine used to do that. You just keep pushing.
script.ja: そういうものよ。うちの子もそうだったわ。あなたはただ、進み続けるだけ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Oh, hey, did you see the 'On This Day' fact for today? I always find those kind of interesting, gives you a bit of perspective.
script.ja: あ、そういえば、今日の「今日は何の日」って見ました？ああいうのっていつも面白いなって思うんですよ、ちょっとした視点を与えてくれるというか。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: I did. The EF5 tornado in North Dakota, last year. June 20, 2025. Quite a thing.
script.ja: ええ、見たわ。去年の6月20日、ノースダコタのEF5トルネードね。すごい出来事だったわ。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: An EF5? Wow. That's the strongest kind, right? I didn't know those still happened. I thought they were, like, historical events you only read about in textbooks.
script.ja: EF5ですか？うわあ。それって一番強いやつですよね？まだそんなのが起きるなんて知りませんでした。なんか、教科書でしか読まない歴史的な出来事だと思ってました。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: They're incredibly rare, thankfully. First one in 12 years, it said. A true anomaly, especially to be that powerful.
script.ja: 幸いなことに、信じられないくらい珍しいのよ。12年ぶりだって書いてあったわ。あれほど強力なのは、まさに異常事態ね。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Man, a tornado like that... imagine if that hit Tokyo. We'd really be in trouble with all this rain. Everything would be flooded, and then a tornado? That's just too much.
script.ja: それにしても、あんな竜巻が…。もし東京を襲ったら、この雨のせいで本当に大変なことになりますね。何もかも水浸しになるだろうし、その上竜巻？それはもう無理ですよ。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Let's hope not. We're not really set up for them here. Our buildings aren't designed for that kind of force, and our infrastructure would struggle.
script.ja: そうならないことを願うわ。ここはあまり竜巻に対応できるようにはできてないから。私たちの建物はああいう力には設計されていないし、インフラも対応しきれないでしょうね。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Yeah, definitely not. My office wouldn't know what to do. They'd probably just send out an email about 'sheltering in place' while the building collapses around us.
script.ja: ええ、確かに。うちの会社じゃどうしていいか分からないでしょうね。建物が周りで崩壊してるのに「その場に留まって避難」とかメールを送るだけですよ。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Sounds about right.
script.ja: そうでしょうね。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: Well, time to face the day. And probably another coffee soon to keep me from falling asleep at my desk during the morning meeting.
script.ja: さて、今日一日頑張りますか。そして、たぶんもうすぐ別のコーヒーを飲んで、朝の会議中にデスクで寝落ちしないようにしないと。

script.speaker: Sarah
script.text: Good luck, Alex. You'll get through it. See you later.
script.ja: 頑張って、アレックス。乗り越えられるわ。また後でね。

script.speaker: Alex
script.text: You too, Sarah. Have a good one.
script.ja: サラさんも。良い一日を。

wikipedia.text: The first EF5 tornado in 12 years occurs in Enderlin, North Dakota.

parenting.title: New Report Reveals Economic and Care Disparities, Continued Mental and Emotional Distress Among Nation’s Babies and Families
parenting.summary: ZERO TO THREE report shows 41% of low-income families saw earnings decline in 2021, and that access to childcare remains a challenge The mental and physical health of our nation’s infants and toddlers continues to be jeopardized by the devastating impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. The fourth annual State of Babies Yearbook: 2022, released today by leading early childhood nonprofit ZERO TO THREE
